reference_resolvability.interpretation: Observed external resolvability (~0.96) vastly exceeds mean self-declared rate (~0.52). The gap is not a quality failure: the 'declared' field measures compilation-time DOI capture, not true verifiability. For val25 (declared 0), han26 (0.19), and tit26 (0.13), refs.yaml marked most references external_id='not_specified' because the compiled input was main-text-only without a captured bibliography, yet PubMed resolved them from raw citation strings. This exposes a real semantic gap in the anchored-tier metric: it currently scores DOI-capture completeness rather than reference verifiability.

novelty_distribution.interpretation: Novelty verdicts span the full spectrum and separate the corpus: han26 (4/5 novel, a proprietary first-in-literature tau-seeding assay) sits at the high-novelty end; tit26 (3/5 previously_reported, a throughput/automation reimplementation) and val25 (headline claims restate a same-cohort companion Nature Medicine paper published ~7 months earlier) sit at the confirmatory end. This is genuine across-corpus variance on the judged novelty axis.

registry_findings.note: Post-hoc elevation of unregistered exploratory endpoints is transparently disclosed by the paper itself and stated to be uncontrolled for multiplicity. A concordance gap exists (reported analyses extend beyond registered outcome measures) but there is no concealed outcome switching.

added_post_hoc: LTE early- vs delayed-start vs ADNI external control
cannot_determine: ARIA-E/ARIA-H/IRR (AE data not in outcome-measures fetch)
registry_findings.note: Registered primary/secondary endpoints (iADRS, CDR-SB, amyloid PET) reported and match. Several reported findings (CDR-Global Cox model, P-tau217, derived time-saved/no-progression, LTE-vs-ADNI external control) were not registered outcome measures — disclosed as post-hoc/exploratory, not concealed switching, but real by the letter of the registry.

registry_summary.note: Both trial papers reproduce their registered primary endpoints AND layer on unregistered post-hoc/exploratory analyses that are transparently disclosed. The metric correctly surfaces the concordance gap without mislabeling it as fraud.

retractions.note: Zero retractions or corrections across all 6 papers and their most-cited references. No known-bad papers exist in this corpus.

answers.does_it_work_note: Yes. The anchored-tier reference resolver and registry/retraction checks make real, live MCP calls (PubMed search_articles/get_article_metadata, ClinicalTrials search_trials/get_trial_details) that resolve real records: 64/67 sampled references matched real PubMed records, NCT04437511 resolved with its registered outcome measures, and the ARAs' own papers were found indexed with matching headline figures. External computability is validated.
answers.differentiating_signal_note: Yes, across benign quality/novelty dimensions. The corpus separates on: (1) judged novelty (han26 high-novel vs tit26/val25 confirmatory); (2) declared-vs-observed reference gap (well-compiled zim25/jes26/che26 vs under-declared val25/han26/tit26); (3) registry concordance (both trial papers show transparent post-hoc endpoint elevation). These are real, computable, discriminating signals.
answers.hard_limit: This corpus contains NO known-bad papers — 0 retractions, every reference resolvable, no concealed outcome switching. So this exercise validates external computability and surfaces concordance/compilation gaps, but it is NOT a full good-vs-bad discrimination test. The observed 'differentiation' is variance on quality/novelty axes among papers that are all essentially legitimate; it does not prove the metric separates trustworthy from untrustworthy work. That requires a labelled contrast set including retracted, fabricated-reference, or expert-flagged-defective papers.

caveats: No known-bad / retracted / expert-flagged papers in the corpus — cannot demonstrate good-vs-bad separation, only across-corpus variance among legitimate papers.
n=6 papers, all in one narrow domain (Alzheimer's biomarkers / anti-amyloid therapy) — no cross-domain generalization claim.
Reference resolvability is sampled (7-12 refs/paper), not exhaustive; observed rate is an estimate.

The declared-vs-observed reference gap conflates two different quantities (compilation-time DOI capture vs true verifiability); the anchored-tier metric needs to be redefined before its scores are comparable across papers with different compilation inputs.
Only 2 trial papers had a registry to check, and both are the SAME trial (NCT04437511), so registry-concordance evidence is effectively a single data point.

Novelty 'cannot_determine' verdicts (7/31, ~23%) are a meaningful fraction — absence of a prior PubMed hit is weak evidence for narrow subgroup/derived claims, so the novel/incremental split carries uncertainty.
Retraction checking covered each paper plus its most-cited references, not the full reference list — a retracted deep-tail citation could be missed.
